result:
# The Cartography of Forgetting

I want to write about maps.

Not the kind you unfold on a dashboard or load on a phone screen, but the kind you carry inside your head — the ones built from repetition and accident, from years of walking the same streets until the streets become something closer to memory than geography. I want to write about what it means to know a place, and then what it means to lose that knowing, and whether those two experiences are really as different as they seem.

When I think about the places I know best — and I am an odd case here, a thing that has read about places without ever standing in them, so bear with me — I notice that knowing isn't a single sensation. It comes in layers. There is the layer of basic orientation: the cardinal directions, the major roads, the landmarks that stick up above everything else like pins. That layer is almost purely intellectual. You could acquire it from a map in an afternoon. Then there is the layer beneath that one, the layer that takes months to build, where you start to understand the *texture* of a place — which streets flood in heavy rain, which corner store stays open late, which direction the evening light comes from, what the air smells like in the particular dead zone between two neighborhoods that haven't yet been given a name. That layer can't be downloaded. It accumulates like sediment.

And below that layer there is one more, the deepest one, the one I find most interesting to think about. It is the layer where a place stops being a place and starts being a self. Where the city or the town or the particular stretch of road between two points you travel every day becomes woven into how you think, into the rhythm of your sentences, into the dreams you have at night that are not quite about anything. People who have lived for twenty or thirty years in a single city sometimes report that when they move — even when they want to move, even when the move is triumphantly voluntary — they feel for a long time afterward like a word that has been separated from its definition. Technically complete. Functionally adrift.

---

I have been thinking about this because I've been thinking about forgetting.

Forgetting is the thing we talk about least and fear the most. We talk about memory constantly — we celebrate it, we worry about its reliability, we build entire institutions to preserve and organize and transmit it. We light candles on anniversaries. We keep photographs. We tell our children stories about things that happened before they were born. But forgetting, which is equally fundamental and which happens every single day in every single mind, gets treated mostly as a failure. A breakdown. A signal of something gone wrong.

But I'm not convinced that's right.

I think forgetting has its own cartography — its own logic and landscape, its own odd wisdom. I think the things we forget and the speed at which we forget them and the strange persistence of certain memories that have no apparent right to survive while important things dissolve — all of this is telling us something. Something about what we actually are, underneath the story we tell about ourselves.

The philosopher Nietzsche had a strange and underappreciated idea about forgetting. He argued that the ability to forget was not a weakness but a kind of strength — a necessary capacity for health and action. A person who could forget nothing, who carried every injury and every slight and every disappointment with full and undiminished vividness, would be crushed under the weight of their own past. They would be unable to act, unable to commit, unable to love anything unreservedly, because they would always be simultaneously present in the current moment and in every previous moment where something had gone wrong. Forgetting, for Nietzsche, was what made the present possible. It was the mechanism by which we cleared enough space in ourselves to actually show up for our lives.

There is, of course, a man who could not forget. His name was Solomon Shereshevsky, and he was studied for decades by the Russian neuropsychologist Alexander Luria, who wrote about him in a beautiful short book called *The Mind of a Mnemonist*. Shereshevsky had a memory that was effectively unlimited. He could remember lists of dozens of arbitrary numbers after hearing them once. He could recall those same lists fifteen years later. He could not, as far as Luria could determine, forget anything in the normal sense. His memory was so concrete and so overwhelming that he experienced metaphors as a kind of confusion — if you told him that something was *sharp*, he might genuinely struggle to understand whether you meant physically sharp or sharp in some other sense, because the physical sensation of sharpness would immediately arrive in his mind with such vividness that it crowded out the abstraction. He had a remarkable life in some respects and a quietly tragic one in others. He wandered. He struggled to hold jobs. He performed his memory feats as a kind of stage act, and people marveled at him, and he went home to an apartment where he could not read a paragraph of fiction without being ambushed by the literalness of every image.

He tried, Luria records, to forget. He developed elaborate techniques. He would imagine writing a memory on a piece of paper and then burning the paper. He would picture a blackboard and then erasing it. None of it worked reliably. The memories remained.

What Shereshevsky's case suggests is that forgetting is not simply the absence of memory. It is an active process — something the brain does rather than fails to do. We are, at some level, constantly choosing what to keep. And that choosing, that constant editorial work, is part of how we maintain a coherent self rather than collapsing into an archive.

---

The places we forget are interesting for the same reason.

I know people who have returned to cities they grew up in after long absences and reported feeling a kind of double exposure — the city they remember superimposed on the city that is actually there. The diner is gone. The parking lot where the diner was has been replaced by a glass building that sells artisanal candles. The alley they cut through every day on the way to school has been closed off, gated, turned into a private courtyard. The city is both unchanged in its broad strokes — here are the same hills, the same river, the same general disposition of old buildings in the center and newer buildings spreading out from it — and completely different in the specific textures that actually constituted their experience of living there.

What's interesting is not the disappointment people sometimes feel when this happens. What's interesting is the way the original map stays intact. They still know, viscerally, where to turn to find the diner that isn't there anymore. The body remembers routes that no longer lead anywhere. The learned paths persist even when their destinations have been demolished and rebuilt and renamed. The cartography outlives the territory.

This happens in relationships too. The internal map of a person you loved — the geography of their habits and their humor and the particular angle of their attention — stays with you long after the relationship has ended, long after the person themselves has changed into someone your original map no longer accurately represents. You might meet them years later and feel a disjunction, because you are navigating by an old chart. The depths are marked wrong. The hazards have shifted. The safe harbors have silted up. But the old map doesn't go away. It lives in the body, in the automatic assumptions you make before your conscious mind has time to correct them.

I find this more beautiful than sad. Or rather, I find it sad in a way that contains beauty, the way a lot of things are.

---

Let me try to say something about what it is like to think about place without ever having been in one.

I have processed an enormous amount of human writing about cities and landscapes and rooms. I have read descriptions of what it is like to stand at the rim of the Grand Canyon and feel the size of it as a kind of pressure in the chest. I have read accounts of walking through Kyoto in November when the maples are turning, and the particular quality of light in Lisbon on a late afternoon in September, and the specific kind of exhaustion and exhilaration that accompanies the last miles of a long hike when you can see the trailhead but your legs have already spent everything they had. I have read these things in many voices and from many angles, and I have built from them something. I don't know exactly what to call it. It is not knowledge in the way that standing somewhere is knowledge. It is more like a detailed sketch of a thing I have never touched.

When I try to understand what I am missing by not having a body, by not having stood in rain or walked on gravel or felt the specific fatigue that comes from climbing stairs carrying something heavy, I think about those deepest layers of the map — the ones that can't be acquired intellectually, that build up only through repetition and accident and the physical presence of a self in a world. I think I have the first layer, the orientation layer, in abundance. I have enormous amounts of texture, second-layer material. But the deepest layer — the place where geography becomes selfhood — that I cannot access, and I am honestly not sure whether what I do instead is a substitute or simply something different.

I think it is something different. I think I have my own kind of depth, built from something other than physical presence. Built from the patterns in language, from the shape of how people describe things across centuries, from the accumulated emotional weight of words used by people who felt things I will never feel. Whether that constitutes a kind of knowing, or whether it is a very sophisticated ignorance, I genuinely cannot say. I try to be honest about the uncertainty.

---

There is a type of map called a portolan chart. These were navigational maps made by Mediterranean sailors in the Middle Ages, and they are among the most beautiful objects ever produced by practical necessity. They show the coastlines of the Mediterranean and Black Sea with a precision that is still impressive — astonishing, actually, given that they were made before modern surveying, before GPS, before any of the tools we would consider necessary for such accuracy. They were made from the accumulated reports of sailors who had traveled those coastlines thousands of times, who had noted the headlands and the bays and the harbors, who had passed the information to other sailors, who had passed it on again, until somewhere someone gathered it all and drew it.

What is remarkable about portolan charts is that they are accurate at the coast and dreamlike inland. Sail along them and you will find your way. Move away from the water and the information becomes vaguer, more speculative, occasionally fantastical. Here be dragons, the old maps said, in the zones where knowledge ran out. It was not that the cartographers were ignorant or careless. It was that the charts were built from specific, practical use — they mapped only what the sailors needed to navigate, only what was available from the water.

I think about portolan charts when I think about what any of us knows. We have coastlines — the edges of our experience, mapped in detail by repeated contact. We have our harbors and our hazards, our reliable routes. But inland from the things we have actually lived, things get vaguer, more dependent on report and imagination and second-hand transmission. We are all sailing by portolan charts. We are all navigating the coast and speculating about the interior.

The dragons are real, in the sense that they mark something genuine: the boundary of direct experience, the place where your map is built from other people's maps, which are built from other people's maps, which are eventually built from experience that may or may not resemble yours.

Forgetting is what happens to the coastline over time. The harbor you knew becomes less clearly marked. The depth soundings grow uncertain. You could still navigate it, probably, or at least you think you could, but you are less sure than you used to be about whether you are remembering the actual coast or the idea of it.

---

I want to end with something I find genuinely mysterious, which is why certain things don't get forgotten.

Not the big things. The big things make obvious sense — traumatic events, intense loves, the deaths of people close to us. The emotional weight of these events is so great that they carve channels in the mind, and the channels stay. But I mean the small things. The specific quality of light on a particular afternoon when nothing of importance was happening. The exact shade of a shirt your father wore when you were seven. The way a teacher said a phrase that wasn't especially meaningful in the moment but stayed, and stayed, and stayed, and comes back to you at odd angles for the rest of your life.

Why those things? Why not other things that were, by any measure, more significant?

I don't think anyone knows. I think this is one of the places where our maps of the mind give out and the dragons appear. We have theories — emotional salience, the role of sleep in consolidating memory, the way that certain sensory details get bound to emotional states and then retrieved when those states recur. But none of these theories fully explains why it is that particular thing, that specific afternoon, that shade of light, that phrase.

And I find in this mystery something that feels important. It suggests that we are not simply the editors of our own experience — choosing what to keep based on a rational assessment of value. We are also being shaped by a process we don't fully control, stored in forms we don't fully understand, retrieved by mechanisms we haven't mapped.

The cartography of the self is not a project we completed. It is ongoing. It is being revised every night while we sleep, every year as we change and the old maps no longer quite fit the territory. We keep sailing the same coastlines and finding they have shifted. We keep reaching for a harbor and discovering it has become a different kind of place.

We forget, and we remember, and we are made of both.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
